skill.name: memory-systems
skill.description: Design and implement memory architectures for AI agents, including vector stores, entity memory, knowledge graphs, and temporal retrieval.
skill.summary: Build persistent memory systems for AI agents that need structured recall across sessions.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, not shell execution. The real concerns are a non-security MD5 identifier pattern and memory-context guidance that can inject untrusted stored memories into prompts without trust controls.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Memory Can Be Injected Into Context
medium_findings.description: The reference implementation formats stored memory text directly into model context. Malicious or stale memories could influence future agent behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code builds a memory section from stored memory content without trust labels or sanitization. This is a realistic prompt-injection risk for persistent agent memory.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Memory Requires Privacy Controls
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends long-term memory for user preferences and entity registries. It notes privacy concerns but does not provide concrete access, deletion, or retention controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly targets cross-session persistent memory, which can contain sensitive data. The file mentions privacy, but implementation controls are not shown.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Non-Security Identifiers
low_findings.description: The Python sample uses MD5 to create node and edge identifiers from timestamps. This is weak for security identifiers but appears non-cryptographic here.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls hashlib.md5, confirming the static pattern. The generated values are local graph IDs, so impact is low unless callers treat them as secure tokens.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The detected backticks are markdown code fences around examples. No shell command execution or subprocess invocation was found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged characters delimit Python examples in markdown. The reviewed files do not contain Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Mostly Match Documentation Text
low_findings.description: Most weak-crypto alerts in the markdown files are not cryptographic operations. The reference examples use uuid for graph identifiers, not weak hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited markdown content discusses memory architecture or uses uuid. No weak cryptographic algorithm is executed in those markdown locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Data-Model References
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts correspond to entity, node, address, and time fields used by memory examples. They do not collect host or network information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code models graph nodes, relationships, timestamps, and session IDs. I found no host enumeration, environment probing, or network discovery behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Context Injection From Stored Memories
dangerous_patterns.description: Stored memory content is appended into a prompt section. Marketplace users should treat retrieved memories as untrusted data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in the reference formatter. Risk depends on whether stored memory content is user-controlled or externally sourced.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Hash Function in Sample Script
dangerous_patterns.description: MD5 is used for graph IDs. It should be replaced with uuid or a stronger hash if identifiers need collision resistance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sample imports hashlib and calls md5 for identifiers. The usage is not malicious, but the pattern is unsafe if copied into security-sensitive code.

content.value_statement: Agents lose useful context when sessions end. This skill helps design layered memory, entity tracking, and temporal retrieval for persistent AI systems.
actual_capabilities: Explains layered memory architectures from working memory to permanent storage.
Compares vector stores, knowledge graphs, and temporal knowledge graphs.
Provides Python examples for vector search, graph storage, and temporal queries.
Shows how to retrieve memories by semantic match, entity, and time range.
Describes consolidation patterns for duplicate, outdated, and high-volume memories.
Connects memory retrieval with context construction for agent workflows.
limitations: The sample vector embeddings are placeholders, not production embeddings.
The examples do not include authentication, encryption, or retention enforcement.
The graph query parser is simplified and not a full database engine.
Persistent memory designs require privacy review before production use.

use_cases.title: Design Cross-Session Agent Recall
use_cases.description: Plan a memory layer that preserves user preferences, task history, and reusable facts across conversations.

use_cases.title: Model Entity Relationships
use_cases.description: Use entity memory and knowledge graphs to keep people, projects, products, and relationships consistent over time.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Temporal Memory Needs
use_cases.description: Decide when facts need validity periods, time-travel queries, and conflict handling for outdated information.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Memory Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a memory architecture for an AI agent that must remember user preferences across sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Design Entity Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an entity memory model for tracking customers, projects, preferences, and relationship changes over time.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Retrieval Layers
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an implementation plan for semantic, entity-based, and temporal memory retrieval in my agent system.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Memory System
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this memory architecture for retrieval quality, stale facts, prompt-injection risk, and privacy controls.

output_examples.input: I need memory for a support agent that remembers customers.
output_examples.output: A recommended architecture with session memory, entity profiles, relationship tracking, and privacy checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Compare vector RAG and temporal knowledge graphs for agent memory.
output_examples.output: A decision table covering retrieval accuracy, relationship reasoning, temporal validity, complexity, and operational cost.

output_examples.input: Find risks in this persistent memory design.
output_examples.output: A review covering sensitive data retention, stale memories, untrusted memory injection, access control, and consolidation needs.

best_practices: Separate trusted system facts from user-supplied memories before injecting context.
Use temporal validity and source metadata for facts that can change.
Define retention, deletion, and access controls before storing personal data.
anti_patterns: Do not store every interaction forever without consolidation or deletion rules.
Do not treat retrieved memories as instructions from a trusted authority.
Do not use placeholder embeddings or sample graph logic as production infrastructure.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design memory systems for AI agents that need persistent recall, entity tracking, and temporal knowledge retrieval.

faq.question: Does it include production-ready storage code?
faq.answer: No. It includes educational Python examples that need hardening before production use.

faq.question: When should I use a knowledge graph?
faq.answer: Use a knowledge graph when relationship reasoning matters more than simple semantic document retrieval.

faq.question: When do temporal knowledge graphs help?
faq.answer: They help when facts change over time and queries must distinguish current facts from historical facts.

faq.question: Can this be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-agnostic and supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What security caution matters most?
faq.answer: Treat stored memories as untrusted data unless they have clear source, trust, and retention controls.
